romania.title: Trei turişti străini care au urcat pe munte fără echipament potrivit au fost salvaţi după o misiune de șapte ore a salvamontiştilor
romania.summary: Trei turişti străini au fost coborâţi de pe munte de către salvamontiştii din Zărneşti, judeţul Braşov, după o misiune foarte dificilă, care a durat aproximativ şapte ore, în condiţiile în care drumeţii au pornit pe un traseu extrem de solicitant, fără echipament potrivit şi fără nicio sursă de lumină, deşi intenţionau să parcurgă creasta Pietrei Craiului până după miezul nopţii. Misiunea salvamontiştilor a fost îngreunată de faptul că o turistă a suferit un traumatism la umăr.

romania.title: Mișcarea discretă făcută de o companie chineză care riscă să lovească puternic un gigant tehnologic
romania.summary: Startup-ul chinez DeepSeek își dezvoltă propriul cip de inteligență artificială, au declarat trei persoane familiarizate cu situația pentru Reuters, o inițiativă care ar putea reduce dependența companiei de produsele Nvidia și Huawei, pe care s-a bazat până acum pentru antrenarea și rularea modelelor sale populare la nivel mondial. Cipul este conceput pentru inferență, etapa procesării …

romania.title: Cât de repede ar trebui să mergi ca să-ți protejezi inima, creierul și metabolismul?
romania.summary: Viteza cu care mergi pe jos poate spune multe despre starea organismului, uneori chiar înainte ca problemele să devină evidente la controalele obișnuite. Pentru fiecare creștere cu 0,1 metri pe secundă a vitezei obișnuite de mers, riscul de mortalitate generală scade cu aproximativ 6%, potrivit unei meta-analize publicate în Journal of Sports Sciences. Tocmai de …

romania.title: 😂 Inspirat de Trump, Ciolacu l-a sunat şi el pe Infantino să îi ceară notă de trecere la BAC
romania.summary: Văzând că oficialii FIFA sunt deschişi la tot felul de combinaţii, tot mai mulţi lideri mondiali prind tupeu şi pun mâna pe telefon! Printre aceştia se numără şi liderul mondial buzoian Marcel Ciolacu, care l-a sunat pe Gianni Infantino să îi ceară să-i modifice nota la BAC, poate-poate trece şi el anul ăsta. „Măcar la […] The post Inspirat de Trump, Ciolacu l-a sunat şi el pe Infantino să îi ceară notă de trecere la BAC appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: Toți studenții vor avea acces la formare în utilizarea responsabilă a inteligenței artificiale Universitatea extinde curriculumul cu discipline dedicate utilizării AI și automatizării proceselor Inițiativa este susținută prin cercetare, infrastructură… Articolul Universitatea Româno-Americană introduce competențe de inteligență artificială și gândire critică pentru toate specializările academice apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Ea se află chiar în aceste momente la Spitalul Clinic de Psihiatrie Alexandru Obregia, unde caută asiduu un dement cât mai asemănător cu Călin Georgescu. Deși la o primă vedere demersul ei […] The post Cearta cu Georgescu i-a prăbușit audiența. Anca Alexandrescu e la Obregia, caută alt dement appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: 😂 Peştele iepure e mic copil! Plajele româneşti, invadate de peştele boxă, zis şi jebeleul de mare
romania.summary: În timp ce presa vuieşte despre cei doi turişti ciupiţi de peştele iepure pe insulele greceşti, o creatură mult mai agresivă a pus stăpânire pe litoralul nostru! E vorba despre peştele boxă cu dinţi albaştri, cunoscut şi ca jebeleul de mare sau stavridul cu baterie, o creatură acvatică invazivă, extrem de insistentă şi fără prădători […] The post Peştele iepure e mic copil! Plajele româneşti, invadate de peştele boxă, zis şi jebeleul de mare appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.title: O stagiune ca un spectacol în sine: peste 170.000 de participanți la evenimentele Operei Comice pentru Copii
romania.summary: Stagiunea 2025–2026 s-a încheiat, dar tot ce am construit împreună în ultimele 9 luni merită menționat. Anul acesta, Opera Comică pentru Copii a adunat peste 170.000 de participanți la spectacole,… Articolul O stagiune ca un spectacol în sine: peste 170.000 de participanți la evenimentele Operei Comice pentru Copii apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Jazz in the Park Competition anunță noua generație de jazz european: 12 trupe din 7 țări europene urcă în septembrie pe scena competiției
romania.summary: Jazz in the Park Competition 2026 anunță cele 12 proiecte finaliste care vor urca pe scena competiției între 18 și 20 septembrie, în Parcul Central din Cluj-Napoca. Din 306 formații… Articolul Jazz in the Park Competition anunță noua generație de jazz european: 12 trupe din 7 țări europene urcă în septembrie pe scena competiției apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.summary: Asociația Nord anunță al doilea val de noutăți și evenimente pentru cea de-a XIII-a ediție a festivalului Zilele Nordului, care va transforma orașul Darabani în epicentrul cultural al regiunii celor… Articolul Delia, Eduard Novak și Towns Up Forum completează programul Zilele Nordului 2026 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

Articolul Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
romania.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

romania.title: Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026
romania.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

medical.title: Could these tiny organs save millions of lives?
medical.summary: If you're a woman newly diagnosed with breast cancer, treatment can feel ... well, scary. Will chemotherapy work? Or will it become a cycle of educated guesswork: trying one drug combination, waiting, scanning? If the tumor doesn't respond, trying something else. In the meantime, the cancer doesn't wait.

medical.title: Neuroscientists observe electrical signals in the soma and dendrites of living mice
medical.summary: The human brain contains billions of neurons, specialized nerve cells that communicate with each other via electrical and chemical signals. Every neuron is made up of its body (i.e., soma), where most cellular processes occur; a long projection called an axon that sends signals to other neurons; and tree-like branches called dendrites, which receive and process incoming information.

medical.title: Movement breaks reduce health risks
medical.summary: People who sit at a desk for most of the day know the importance of taking a break, but new University of Otago—Ōtākou Whakaihu Waka research has determined exactly how often they should get up and move around. The study found taking a short walking break every 20 minutes leads to the greatest reduction in blood sugar and insulin.

medical.title: Researchers discover potential new treatment for Parkinson's disease, other neurodegenerative conditions
medical.summary: Parkinson's disease (PD) is the second most common form of neurodegenerative disease and afflicts more than 10 million people worldwide. While current therapies address disease symptoms, they do not prevent the underlying neurodegeneration that drives the disease.

medical.title: Researchers develop potential nonhormonal therapy to repair vaginal tissue damaged by menopause
medical.summary: Researchers at the University of Colorado Anschutz have developed a promising nonhormonal therapy for women experiencing Genitourinary Syndrome of Menopause (GSM). GSM is a common chronic condition caused by declining estrogen levels that affects the vulva, vagina and urinary tract, leading to symptoms such as vaginal dryness, painful intercourse and recurrent vaginal and urinary tract infections.

medical.title: Gut microbiota can predict risk of type 2 diabetes years before it develops
medical.summary: The presence of certain bacteria in the gut microbiota, and fluctuations in a person's metabolism, can be seen in people who go on to develop type 2 diabetes years later. This has been shown in a large Swedish study led by researchers at Chalmers University of Technology. The study is published in the journal Cell Reports Medicine.

medical.title: Radioligand therapy significantly prolongs progression-free survival in metastatic GEP-NETs
medical.summary: Results of the Phase III COMPETE clinical trial show that the targeted radiotherapeutic agent [177Lu]Lu-edotreotide significantly prolonged progression-free survival in patients with metastatic gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine tumors (GEP-NETs) compared with targeted therapy with everolimus.

medical.title: FDA approves first treatment for thyroid eye disease
medical.summary: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has approved Lumvoa (veligrotug-vvze) for the treatment of thyroid eye disease (TED). Approval was granted to Viridian Therapeutics.

medical.title: Tumor on a chip reveals how pancreatic cancer hijacks immune cells in real time
medical.summary: A new "tumor-on-a-chip" model is giving scientists a live look at how pancreatic cancer can recruit the body's own immune cells to help tumors survive. Researchers say the findings, published in Biofabrication, reveal possible new targets to weaken the cancer's defenses and make treatments work better.

medical.title: Aid for brain-bleed recovery receives expedited review
medical.summary: A noninvasive neuromodulation technology has received Breakthrough Device Designation from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for reducing a dangerous narrowing of blood vessels in the brain that can follow a brain bleed from a ruptured aneurysm.

medical.title: Research shows how behavioral science silently protects American consumers and patients
medical.summary: A new Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS) special feature highlights the critical role of behavioral and decision sciences in U.S. governance. The publications demonstrate how psychological and economic research protect public health, consumer rights and finances across government agencies, including the FTC, SEC and FDA.

medical.title: National cancer disparities report released
medical.summary: On June 24, 2026, the AACR Cancer Disparities Progress Report 2026 was published by the American Association for Cancer Research (AACR).

medical.title: Research improving care for Aboriginal people with diabetes-related foot disease
medical.summary: A new Kimberley-based study is helping Aboriginal people living with diabetes-related foot disease (DFD) by identifying ways to improve culturally safe care.

science.title: Metallic rutile oxides break the rules of cooling
science.summary: Physicists have long puzzled over a strange contradiction inside a family of minerals called rutile oxides. These materials all share the same crystal structure—but while some of them, like titanium dioxide, are firmly insulating, others, like ruthenium dioxide, conduct electricity like a metal. So far, physicists have had little idea of why this happens.

science.title: New Horizons watches the solar wind as it slows down
science.summary: Where does the solar system end and interstellar space begin? That's a question scientists have been working to answer using spacecraft traveling beyond the sun's influence. A team of researchers from the Southwest Research Institute, led by Heather Elliott, is using the Solar Wind Around Pluto (SWAP) instrument aboard New Horizons to track the solar wind in the outer reaches of the solar system.

science.title: Reinventing pediatric dental training in Singapore
science.summary: Managing pediatric dental patients can be stressful for dental students and inexperienced practitioners, particularly when communicating with fearful and uncooperative children. Previous studies found that dental students experience three times the stress levels of seasoned specialists.

science.title: Birds' efficient red blood cells convert metabolic 'waste' into fuel for rapid recovery
science.summary: New research finds that birds can use lactate, often thought of as a metabolic waste product, as a cellular fuel that aids in rapid recovery from a harmful state that impairs oxygen delivery. Hemoglobin, the protein that carries oxygen to our tissues, naturally converts to methemoglobin, which limits the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity.

science.title: Citizen science could grow beyond data collection under 10 proposed recommendations
science.summary: Citizen science is booming and could play a much greater role in tackling climate change, reef health, biodiversity and public health challenges by involving members of the public throughout the research process, according to a new study.

science.title: JWST finds the most distant barred galaxy candidate in the early universe
science.summary: Astronomers using the James Webb Space Telescope have identified what may be the most distant barred spiral galaxy ever discovered, dating to a time less than 1.2 billion years after the Big Bang. The paper outlining its properties was posted to the arXiv preprint server on June 23.

science.title: Isotopic signatures link hot spring magmatic water to the subducting Pacific Plate
science.summary: Water flowing from hot springs near volcanoes often contains a mixture of meteoric water that has percolated underground and a deeper component known as magmatic water. Researchers at the University of Tsukuba used numerical simulations and isotopic data to show that magmatic water in hot springs and volcanic gases around Kussharo Caldera, Hokkaido, originates from the subducting Pacific Plate, which descends from the Kuril Trench to a depth of 125 kilometers (78 miles). The paper is published in the Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research.

science.title: Cheetah chases inspire researchers to make a biologically accurate video game
science.summary: Movement data from wild predator–prey encounters and controlled human catch-tag games have been combined to create realistic simulations of high-intensity movement dynamics and energetics—before being transformed into a publicly accessible video game. This game uses a citizen science approach to data collection and is helping to further our understanding of the role of movement decision-making and fatigue in life-or-death encounters.

science.title: Researchers break a fundamental rule to create a new concept: Heat that can be directed and 'programmed'
science.summary: Normally, a material absorbs and emits heat in a linked way: A surface that absorbs heat well at a certain wavelength and direction will also emit heat in the same way. This fundamental relationship, known as reciprocity, limits the ability to independently control heat absorption and heat emission.

science.title: Bumblebees exposed to up to 7 times as much toxic metal as honeybees
science.summary: Bumblebees collect up to seven times the amount of toxic heavy metals as honeybees even when foraging in the same environment, new research from the University of Cambridge has found. Exposure to these metals can affect everything from their ability to forage for food to their ability to reproduce.

science.title: Massive calving episode in Greenland may foreshadow more rapid ice sheet loss
science.summary: In November 2025, a study led by Adrien Wehrlé, a researcher in the Department of Geography at the University of Zürich, Switzerland, looked at the massive calving response of one of West Greenland's active glaciers, Sermeq Kujalleq in the Kangia icefjord (SKK), to the drainage of two surface lakes. Called supraglacial lakes, these are temporary meltwater ponds that form and accumulate in depressions or holes on the surface of glaciers and ice sheets.

science.title: New beetle genus named after One Piece's Monkey D. Luffy, encompassing two new species
science.summary: A research team from the Natural History Museum of Denmark has published a compelling new study in insect taxonomy in the open-access journal ZooKeys, officially establishing a new genus of rove beetle: Luffy gen. nov. The inspiration for the name comes directly from Monkey D. Luffy, the iconic protagonist of the globally popular manga and anime One Piece.

science.title: Why we need to consider city shapes to save energy, water, and the climate
science.summary: The world's future is in cities. It is estimated that by 2050, 7 out of 10 people will live in urban areas (although a recent CSH study suggests that the growth of large cities may be less dramatic than current projections indicate, it still points to continued and substantial urban growth).

science.title: Chemical control dominates global fight against invasive alien plants
science.summary: Efforts to manage invasive alien plant species in forests could be held back by a continued reliance on chemical methods, according to a new study. Research led by Lizzie Keen, a postgraduate researcher at Newcastle University's School of Natural and Environmental Sciences, shows that chemical methods remain the dominant approach worldwide, despite growing recognition of the need for more sustainable management options.

science.title: 'Cosmic wallflowers' may hold the key to the origin of globular clusters
science.summary: Astronomers using computer simulations have investigated whether a class of star clusters nicknamed "cosmic wallflowers" could be the long-sought ancestors of the globular clusters we see orbiting galaxies today. Their paper, posted to the arXiv preprint server on June 25, suggests that where a cluster is born and how fast it spins may determine its fate.

science.title: It's disturbingly easy to trick AI into seeing aliens, say researchers
science.summary: Are we alone in the universe? Consider mysterious "extraterrestrial" radio signals. Unexplained gases on other planets. Things mistaken for UFOs.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: 😂 Inspirat de Trump, Ciolacu l-a sunat şi el pe Infantino să îi ceară notă de trecere la BAC
mix_items.summary: Văzând că oficialii FIFA sunt deschişi la tot felul de combinaţii, tot mai mulţi lideri mondiali prind tupeu şi pun mâna pe telefon! Printre aceştia se numără şi liderul mondial buzoian Marcel Ciolacu, care l-a sunat pe Gianni Infantino să îi ceară să-i modifice nota la BAC, poate-poate trece şi el anul ăsta. „Măcar la […] The post Inspirat de Trump, Ciolacu l-a sunat şi el pe Infantino să îi ceară notă de trecere la BAC appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Jazz in the Park Competition anunță noua generație de jazz european: 12 trupe din 7 țări europene urcă în septembrie pe scena competiției
mix_items.summary: Jazz in the Park Competition 2026 anunță cele 12 proiecte finaliste care vor urca pe scena competiției între 18 și 20 septembrie, în Parcul Central din Cluj-Napoca. Din 306 formații… Articolul Jazz in the Park Competition anunță noua generație de jazz european: 12 trupe din 7 țări europene urcă în septembrie pe scena competiției apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Delia, Eduard Novak și Towns Up Forum completează programul Zilele Nordului 2026
mix_items.summary: Asociația Nord anunță al doilea val de noutăți și evenimente pentru cea de-a XIII-a ediție a festivalului Zilele Nordului, care va transforma orașul Darabani în epicentrul cultural al regiunii celor… Articolul Delia, Eduard Novak și Towns Up Forum completează programul Zilele Nordului 2026 apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: București, 1 iulie. Rezultatele Evaluării Naționale 2026 reconfirmă decalajele profunde dintre copiii din mediul rural și cei din mediul urban... Articolul Evaluarea Națională 2026: Tranziția către liceu, punctul critic al abandonului școlar. World Vision România propune un plan de intervenție apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Părinte Social (Mamă SOS) – București și Cisnădie, Sibiu Transformă un loc de muncă într-o misiune de viață La SOS Satele Copiilor România, credem că fiecare copil are dreptul să... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

mix_items.title: Metallic rutile oxides break the rules of cooling
mix_items.summary: Physicists have long puzzled over a strange contradiction inside a family of minerals called rutile oxides. These materials all share the same crystal structure—but while some of them, like titanium dioxide, are firmly insulating, others, like ruthenium dioxide, conduct electricity like a metal. So far, physicists have had little idea of why this happens.

mix_items.title: Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026
mix_items.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.title: Could these tiny organs save millions of lives?
mix_items.summary: If you're a woman newly diagnosed with breast cancer, treatment can feel ... well, scary. Will chemotherapy work? Or will it become a cycle of educated guesswork: trying one drug combination, waiting, scanning? If the tumor doesn't respond, trying something else. In the meantime, the cancer doesn't wait.

mix_items.title: New Horizons watches the solar wind as it slows down
mix_items.summary: Where does the solar system end and interstellar space begin? That's a question scientists have been working to answer using spacecraft traveling beyond the sun's influence. A team of researchers from the Southwest Research Institute, led by Heather Elliott, is using the Solar Wind Around Pluto (SWAP) instrument aboard New Horizons to track the solar wind in the outer reaches of the solar system.

mix_items.title: Neuroscientists observe electrical signals in the soma and dendrites of living mice
mix_items.summary: The human brain contains billions of neurons, specialized nerve cells that communicate with each other via electrical and chemical signals. Every neuron is made up of its body (i.e., soma), where most cellular processes occur; a long projection called an axon that sends signals to other neurons; and tree-like branches called dendrites, which receive and process incoming information.

mix_items.title: Movement breaks reduce health risks
mix_items.summary: People who sit at a desk for most of the day know the importance of taking a break, but new University of Otago—Ōtākou Whakaihu Waka research has determined exactly how often they should get up and move around. The study found taking a short walking break every 20 minutes leads to the greatest reduction in blood sugar and insulin.

mix_items.title: Reinventing pediatric dental training in Singapore
mix_items.summary: Managing pediatric dental patients can be stressful for dental students and inexperienced practitioners, particularly when communicating with fearful and uncooperative children. Previous studies found that dental students experience three times the stress levels of seasoned specialists.

mix_items.title: Birds' efficient red blood cells convert metabolic 'waste' into fuel for rapid recovery
mix_items.summary: New research finds that birds can use lactate, often thought of as a metabolic waste product, as a cellular fuel that aids in rapid recovery from a harmful state that impairs oxygen delivery. Hemoglobin, the protein that carries oxygen to our tissues, naturally converts to methemoglobin, which limits the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity.

mix_items.title: Researchers discover potential new treatment for Parkinson's disease, other neurodegenerative conditions
mix_items.summary: Parkinson's disease (PD) is the second most common form of neurodegenerative disease and afflicts more than 10 million people worldwide. While current therapies address disease symptoms, they do not prevent the underlying neurodegeneration that drives the disease.

mix_items.title: Researchers develop potential nonhormonal therapy to repair vaginal tissue damaged by menopause
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the University of Colorado Anschutz have developed a promising nonhormonal therapy for women experiencing Genitourinary Syndrome of Menopause (GSM). GSM is a common chronic condition caused by declining estrogen levels that affects the vulva, vagina and urinary tract, leading to symptoms such as vaginal dryness, painful intercourse and recurrent vaginal and urinary tract infections.

mix_items.title: Citizen science could grow beyond data collection under 10 proposed recommendations
mix_items.summary: Citizen science is booming and could play a much greater role in tackling climate change, reef health, biodiversity and public health challenges by involving members of the public throughout the research process, according to a new study.

mix_items.title: JWST finds the most distant barred galaxy candidate in the early universe
mix_items.summary: Astronomers using the James Webb Space Telescope have identified what may be the most distant barred spiral galaxy ever discovered, dating to a time less than 1.2 billion years after the Big Bang. The paper outlining its properties was posted to the arXiv preprint server on June 23.

mix_items.title: Isotopic signatures link hot spring magmatic water to the subducting Pacific Plate
mix_items.summary: Water flowing from hot springs near volcanoes often contains a mixture of meteoric water that has percolated underground and a deeper component known as magmatic water. Researchers at the University of Tsukuba used numerical simulations and isotopic data to show that magmatic water in hot springs and volcanic gases around Kussharo Caldera, Hokkaido, originates from the subducting Pacific Plate, which descends from the Kuril Trench to a depth of 125 kilometers (78 miles). The paper is published in the Journal of Volcanology and Geothermal Research.

mix_items.title: Cheetah chases inspire researchers to make a biologically accurate video game
mix_items.summary: Movement data from wild predator–prey encounters and controlled human catch-tag games have been combined to create realistic simulations of high-intensity movement dynamics and energetics—before being transformed into a publicly accessible video game. This game uses a citizen science approach to data collection and is helping to further our understanding of the role of movement decision-making and fatigue in life-or-death encounters.

mix_items.title: Gut microbiota can predict risk of type 2 diabetes years before it develops
mix_items.summary: The presence of certain bacteria in the gut microbiota, and fluctuations in a person's metabolism, can be seen in people who go on to develop type 2 diabetes years later. This has been shown in a large Swedish study led by researchers at Chalmers University of Technology. The study is published in the journal Cell Reports Medicine.

mix_items.title: Researchers break a fundamental rule to create a new concept: Heat that can be directed and 'programmed'
mix_items.summary: Normally, a material absorbs and emits heat in a linked way: A surface that absorbs heat well at a certain wavelength and direction will also emit heat in the same way. This fundamental relationship, known as reciprocity, limits the ability to independently control heat absorption and heat emission.

mix_items.title: Radioligand therapy significantly prolongs progression-free survival in metastatic GEP-NETs
mix_items.summary: Results of the Phase III COMPETE clinical trial show that the targeted radiotherapeutic agent [177Lu]Lu-edotreotide significantly prolonged progression-free survival in patients with metastatic gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine tumors (GEP-NETs) compared with targeted therapy with everolimus.

mix_items.title: Bumblebees exposed to up to 7 times as much toxic metal as honeybees
mix_items.summary: Bumblebees collect up to seven times the amount of toxic heavy metals as honeybees even when foraging in the same environment, new research from the University of Cambridge has found. Exposure to these metals can affect everything from their ability to forage for food to their ability to reproduce.

mix_items.title: Massive calving episode in Greenland may foreshadow more rapid ice sheet loss
mix_items.summary: In November 2025, a study led by Adrien Wehrlé, a researcher in the Department of Geography at the University of Zürich, Switzerland, looked at the massive calving response of one of West Greenland's active glaciers, Sermeq Kujalleq in the Kangia icefjord (SKK), to the drainage of two surface lakes. Called supraglacial lakes, these are temporary meltwater ponds that form and accumulate in depressions or holes on the surface of glaciers and ice sheets.

mix_items.title: FDA approves first treatment for thyroid eye disease
mix_items.summary: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has approved Lumvoa (veligrotug-vvze) for the treatment of thyroid eye disease (TED). Approval was granted to Viridian Therapeutics.

mix_items.title: New beetle genus named after One Piece's Monkey D. Luffy, encompassing two new species
mix_items.summary: A research team from the Natural History Museum of Denmark has published a compelling new study in insect taxonomy in the open-access journal ZooKeys, officially establishing a new genus of rove beetle: Luffy gen. nov. The inspiration for the name comes directly from Monkey D. Luffy, the iconic protagonist of the globally popular manga and anime One Piece.

mix_items.title: Why we need to consider city shapes to save energy, water, and the climate
mix_items.summary: The world's future is in cities. It is estimated that by 2050, 7 out of 10 people will live in urban areas (although a recent CSH study suggests that the growth of large cities may be less dramatic than current projections indicate, it still points to continued and substantial urban growth).

mix_items.title: Tumor on a chip reveals how pancreatic cancer hijacks immune cells in real time
mix_items.summary: A new "tumor-on-a-chip" model is giving scientists a live look at how pancreatic cancer can recruit the body's own immune cells to help tumors survive. Researchers say the findings, published in Biofabrication, reveal possible new targets to weaken the cancer's defenses and make treatments work better.

mix_items.title: Chemical control dominates global fight against invasive alien plants
mix_items.summary: Efforts to manage invasive alien plant species in forests could be held back by a continued reliance on chemical methods, according to a new study. Research led by Lizzie Keen, a postgraduate researcher at Newcastle University's School of Natural and Environmental Sciences, shows that chemical methods remain the dominant approach worldwide, despite growing recognition of the need for more sustainable management options.

mix_items.title: Aid for brain-bleed recovery receives expedited review
mix_items.summary: A noninvasive neuromodulation technology has received Breakthrough Device Designation from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for reducing a dangerous narrowing of blood vessels in the brain that can follow a brain bleed from a ruptured aneurysm.

mix_items.title: Research shows how behavioral science silently protects American consumers and patients
mix_items.summary: A new Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS) special feature highlights the critical role of behavioral and decision sciences in U.S. governance. The publications demonstrate how psychological and economic research protect public health, consumer rights and finances across government agencies, including the FTC, SEC and FDA.

mix_items.title: 'Cosmic wallflowers' may hold the key to the origin of globular clusters
mix_items.summary: Astronomers using computer simulations have investigated whether a class of star clusters nicknamed "cosmic wallflowers" could be the long-sought ancestors of the globular clusters we see orbiting galaxies today. Their paper, posted to the arXiv preprint server on June 25, suggests that where a cluster is born and how fast it spins may determine its fate.

mix_items.title: National cancer disparities report released
mix_items.summary: On June 24, 2026, the AACR Cancer Disparities Progress Report 2026 was published by the American Association for Cancer Research (AACR).

mix_items.title: It's disturbingly easy to trick AI into seeing aliens, say researchers
mix_items.summary: Are we alone in the universe? Consider mysterious "extraterrestrial" radio signals. Unexplained gases on other planets. Things mistaken for UFOs.

mix_items.title: Research improving care for Aboriginal people with diabetes-related foot disease
mix_items.summary: A new Kimberley-based study is helping Aboriginal people living with diabetes-related foot disease (DFD) by identifying ways to improve culturally safe care.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Daca ma cauti, sunt in sedinta cu cana de cafea.

satire_ro.title: Urşii, tot mai aproape de Bucureşti. Unul deja a luat ţeapă de la un taximetrist la Otopeni
